decision_rows.evidence: pqc_anchor.c stores the persistent hardware anchor as an authenticated prefix record in a pre-provisioned TPM NV index using owner-hierarchy nvread/nvwrite commands.  It contains no PCR policy-session, sealed-object, or unseal path.
decision_rows.paper_gate_phrase: PCR binding is a transient probe rather than a persistent filesystem policy

decision_rows.evidence: The PCR probe artifact seals a transient object, unseals under the current PCR digest, and rejects a drifted PCR digest; its own note says it is not a filesystem freshness proof.
decision_rows.paper_gate_phrase: not a claim that the persistent filesystem anchor itself is PCR-sealed

decision_rows.evidence: The main paper gates the claim in design, evaluation, security, abstract, and conclusion wording; all high-risk PCR mentions are in a negated, transient, or remaining-gap context.

high_risk_pcr_mentions.context: The per-file envelope is authenticated and block records are AEAD-protected, but the mount password remains the root credential. The implementation has no hardware-backed credential release, audited rotation protocol, privileged-attacker defense, or side-channel evaluation. File anchors are replayable. TPM mode requires an administrator-provisioned index; retained probes cover fail-closed replay-after-advance and transient PCR-policy behavior, not persistent PCR-bound key release.

The per-file envelope is authenticated and block records are AEAD-protected, but the mount password remains the root credential. The implementation has no hardware-backed credential release, audited rotation protocol, privileged-attacker defense, or side-channel evaluation. File anchors are replayable. TPM mode requires an administrator-provisioned index; retained probes cover fail-closed replay-after-advance and transient PCR-policy behavior, not persistent PCR-bound key release.

The paper is intentionally conservative about unsupported mechanisms. It does not claim verified \texttt{O\_DIRECT} NVMe-to-UVM DMA, an \texttt{io\_uring}/eBPF completion bypass, GPU constant-time execution, persistent PCR-bound freshness, true power-loss crash certification, or portability beyond the tested stack. These exclusions are not a substitute for contributions; they define the boundary within which the measured system should be read.

The TPM artifacts record anchor latency, fixed properties, PCR reads, NV public attributes for index \texttt{0x01500010}, and a transient PCR-policy probe that unseals under the current PCR digest but rejects a drifted digest. The replay-after-advance harness restores a stale backing-directory snapshot after the TPM-backed anchor advances and observes \texttt{fail\_closed} at payload read time. This is replay-after-advance evidence, not a claim that the persistent filesystem anchor itself is PCR

AEGIS-Q is an evidence-first hybrid cryptographic-storage prototype: a remountable FUSE encrypted-block format with CPU-first AES-GCM, optional batched GPU key-plane maintenance, TPM replay-after-advance evidence, SQLite/\texttt{dbm.dumb} recovery artifacts, and mounted-FUSE throttle traces on a Jetson AGX Thor. It still does not claim direct NVMe-to-UVM DMA, eBPF/io\_uring completion bypass, GPU constant-time behavior, persistent PCR-bound freshness, power-loss crash certification, foreground A

The older \texttt{pqc\_file\_key.*} epoch/grace helper remains source-only and is not linked into \texttt{pqc\_fuse}; evaluated epochs are open-file refresh bookkeeping, not stale-handle, rollback, or TPM/PCR-bound freshness proof.

The hardware mode stores an authenticated prefix record in an owner-authorized TPM NV index: administrators create and rotate the index outside the daemon, and AEGIS-Q reads or writes only that record. PCR binding is a transient probe rather than a persistent filesystem policy; a software update or PCR change therefore requires explicit reprovisioning/resealing. Backup or migration cannot copy only the backing directory; it needs a fresh authorized anchor bootstrap. Lost mount credentials are un

\begin{document} \begin{abstract} Unified-memory edge devices increasingly co-locate foreground inference, local databases, and long-lived encrypted state on the same DRAM and storage fabric. A secure-storage system for this setting must therefore do more than encrypt blocks: it must place cryptographic work where it does not disrupt latency-sensitive tenants, publish durable authenticated state without nonce reuse, and avoid relying on undocumented accelerator/storage semantics. AEGIS-Q is a FU

non_claims: no PCR-bound backup or restore protocol
scope: The persistent filesystem anchor is not PCR-bound in this revision. PCR evidence is retained only as a transient policy probe; persistent freshness evidence is limited to pre-provisioned TPM NV replay-after-advance fail-closed behavior.
